lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market operators that provide recurring outsourced gift-shop or merchandise-program services to attractions, museums, zoos, aquariums, historic sites, hospitality properties, destinations, campuses, airports, or comparable venue partners, including store operation, product development and sourcing, merchandising, staffing, fulfillment, and branded e-commerce.
lens.excluded_activities: Standalone consumer gift, greeting-card, novelty, holiday, and souvenir shops without an external venue or institutional service contract; one-off product sales; artisan microbusinesses; pure wholesalers; captive venue-owned stores; shells; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The operator assumes responsibility for a partner venue's retail program and earns product gross margin under a multiyear operating, concession, lease, or revenue-share arrangement. The venue may receive a percentage of gross sales or minimum guarantee, while visitors buy in-store or online merchandise tied to the venue's mission, destination, or brand.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to outsourced experiential-retail and merchandise-program operators because NAICS 459420 is primarily transactional merchandise retail, whereas ordinary standalone gift shops do not supply an outsourced service. The distinction is necessary for a coherent screen and is not an attractiveness selection.

a.bridge: Data USA reports 137,931 workers in the predecessor gift, novelty, and souvenir shops group in 2022, including 40,718 retail salespersons, 30,939 first-line retail-sales supervisors, and 16,916 cashiers. O*NET lists retail-sales tasks from customer discovery and recommendations through payment, inventory requisition, records, special orders, display, packaging, and loss prevention. I assign high AI exposure to routine product content, marketing, records, purchasing analysis, forecasting, scheduling, inquiry, and e-commerce support; medium exposure to assisted selling, product ideation, and replenishment; and low exposure to physical display, stocking, packaging, construction, guest engagement, and accountable onsite supervision, then apply a wage-weighted judgment. The occupation source covers all gift retailers and may understate central product, e-commerce, and analytics roles at multisite outsourced operators, biasing exposure downward; it may also overstate cashier exposure if self-checkout is already installed.
a.rationale: AI can reduce work in assortment research, product descriptions, localized design concepts, demand forecasting, purchase-order support, scheduling, digital marketing, customer inquiries, and partner reporting. The model remains heavily dependent on onsite guest service, visual merchandising, stock handling, store operations, vendor coordination, product judgment, and venue-brand stewardship, limiting substitution.
a.change_evidence: Firm payroll and wage files mapped to task-level time studies across headquarters, buying, design, partner reporting, e-commerce, store management, sales, cashier, stock, and fulfillment roles would materially move a; pre/post labor telemetry from actual AI deployments at attraction retailers would test current automation.
caveats: The occupation distribution uses the broader predecessor industry and is not specific to outsourced venue operators.
Task exposure is judgmentally mapped and does not measure technically feasible substitution in this industry.

The frozen compensation-to-receipts input is at ancestor 44-45, has a 2024-wage/2022-receipts vintage mismatch, and is LOW quality; it may materially under- or overstate the narrowed multisite service model.

rho.rationale: Cloud POS, e-commerce, inventory, digital marketing, and partner-reporting systems provide usable workflow surfaces for AI within five years. Implementation is constrained by fragmented SKU and vendor data, seasonal and location-specific demand, small central teams, PCI and privacy controls, integration cost, brand approvals, creative-quality risk, labor turnover, and the continuing need for onsite execution.
rho.change_evidence: Implementation case studies from Event Network, airport concessionaires, museum-shop operators, and their POS or merchandise-planning vendors showing labor hours, stockouts, conversion, forecast accuracy, content throughput, and error rates before and after AI deployment would materially move rho; 15 operator interviews could test integration readiness.
caveats: This is bounded judgment, not an observed adoption cohort.
The documented turnkey operator is likely more technologically capable than an LMM operator.
Demand, pricing, concession economics, and valuation are excluded from rho.

e.rationale: Census defines the industry around retailing gifts, novelty merchandise, souvenirs, cards, and seasonal goods, so most firms are standalone product sellers outside the service lens. The qualifying model requires an external venue contract plus an integrated ability to operate stores, source and develop merchandise, staff locations, report to partners, and often run e-commerce. Event Network documents that model across more than 100 partnerships, proving existence but not broad prevalence; eligibility is therefore estimated as a small minority.
e.change_evidence: A US business-universe file for NAICS 459420 linked to venue-management contracts, concession awards, store counts, customer concentration, EBITDA, and independence status would measure the numerator; Museum Store Association, airport, zoo and aquarium association rosters plus procurement-award databases could identify outsourced versus captive operations.
caveats: No source measures the share of LMM firms that are outsourced operators.
Some qualifying operators may be classified under another retail, concession, management, or services NAICS code.
The frozen n input is a declared dataset gap and will be MISSING, so this packet does not infer an eligible firm count.

s5.bridge: Gallup's fall-2024 survey of US business owners reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership in the next five years, while 52.3% of US employer businesses were owned by people 55 or older. BizBuySell documents completed sales across broad independent retail from 2021-2025. I discount the 22% stated-plan rate because plans do not always complete, gifts and some internal transfers are not qualifying control sales, and neither source isolates gift-shop concession operators or the LMM EBITDA band. Conversely, contract portfolios and operating infrastructure should make eligible multisite operators more transferable than owner-personal artisan shops; the net direction of proxy error is uncertain.
s5.rationale: Eligible employer operators have contracts, staff, systems, and partner relationships that can support transfer, while venue consent, customer concentration, concession renewal risk, working-capital seasonality, and a small strategic-buyer universe can prevent closing. The base is below the general employer-owner plan rate and represents completed qualifying control transfers, not intent.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, and broker records for five years of gift, museum, attraction, campus, hotel, and airport retail operators, matched to a denominator of eligible LMM firms and coded for completed control transfer, closure, failed sale, family gift, and internal reorganization, would directly estimate s5.
caveats: Gallup spans all industries and firm sizes, and stated plans are not completed transactions.
BizBuySell retail businesses are generally far smaller than the specified EBITDA band and are not industry-specific.
Venue contracts may require consent or rebidding upon change of control, reducing transferability.

q.bridge: A public Griffith Observatory bookstore and gift-shop concession agreement applies revenue-sharing rates of 25% to 38% of gross receipts by sales tier, while a 2025 US airport gift-shop agreement uses the greater of a minimum annual guarantee or a percentage of gross revenue and constrains pricing. These agreements do not share operator cost savings directly, so implemented labor benefit can be retained during the term; however, revenue shares, guarantees, service standards, open bidding, and renewal negotiations allow venues and customers to capture value over time. Public/high-traffic concessions may impose higher fees and more formal bidding than smaller attraction partnerships, which likely biases inferred pass-through upward.
q.rationale: Because the operator sells merchandise rather than billable labor, an automation saving initially remains in merchandise margin and does not automatically reduce the venue's percentage of sales. Over five years, contract renewal, competing bids, minimum guarantees, partner demands, and retail price competition should transfer a substantial portion through higher venue economics, better service, or lower prices.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of museum, zoo, hospitality, campus, and airport operating agreements showing revenue share, guarantees, expense pass-through, renewal terms, pricing constraints, change-of-control clauses, and rebid outcomes, paired with margin waterfalls after automation, would materially move q.
caveats: The cited concession documents are venue-specific and not a distribution of LMM contract terms.
Revenue share as a percentage of sales is not the same as sharing an AI-generated gross benefit.
Volume loss and implementation difficulty are excluded and represented in d5, o, and rho.

d5.bridge: The Spring 2026 U.S. Travel forecast projects inflation-adjusted travel spending growth of 1% in 2026 and 3% in both 2027 and 2028, with domestic leisure spending at $909 billion in 2026 and growing faster thereafter through a 2030 horizon. NPS recorded more than 323 million recreation visits in 2025, while the American Alliance of Museums reports uneven attendance recovery. I translate travel and attraction visitation into the quantity of current outsourced experiential-retail services, allowing for lower souvenir conversion, weak museums, consumer trade-down, and venue mix. Travel spending is broader than shop transactions and may overstate gift-shop quantity if more spend shifts to lodging, transport, or experiences; resilient visitation may understate exposure to merchandise e-commerce and discretionary budgets.
d5.rationale: Attraction and destination attendance supports physical souvenir and gift purchases, and real domestic leisure spending is forecast to grow. Demand is discretionary and vulnerable to consumer confidence, international travel, venue attendance, tariffs, and substitution toward experiences or online purchases. The base case is modest real quantity growth rather than a direct copy of travel spending growth.
d5.change_evidence: Same-store units, transactions, conversion rates, items per visitor, and constant-price sales from museum, zoo, park, resort, campus, and airport shop cohorts through 2028, combined with NPS, AAM, TSA, hotel, and venue-attendance forecasts, would materially narrow d5.
caveats: Travel spending is not gift-shop unit demand and includes price and unrelated categories.
The published travel forecast ends in 2030, one year short of the requested year-five endpoint from 2026.
Museum recovery and international travel remain uneven, creating material venue-level dispersion.

o.bridge: Event Network says it takes responsibility for every aspect of attraction retail, including store design, people, guest service, operations, product development, purchasing, merchandising, e-commerce, fulfillment, technology, and partner collaboration, and reports a 97% partnership renewal rate across more than 100 partnerships. I use the breadth of accountable physical and brand-sensitive tasks and renewal evidence to infer a high operator-required share, then reduce it for venue insourcing, direct-to-consumer brands, self-checkout, marketplace purchases, automated e-commerce, and eliminated low-value retail. The renewal statistic is vendor-reported and likely selected from a successful scaled operator, biasing persistence upward.
o.rationale: Year-five merchandise still needs licensed product decisions, sourcing, inventory ownership, store staffing, visual merchandising, fulfillment, revenue reporting, brand compliance, and service recovery. Software can automate portions but cannot supply physical goods or own venue performance. Some venues can insource or replace a contractor, so the external operator-required share remains high but below one.
o.change_evidence: Cohort data on outsourced-contract renewals, insourcing, closures, e-commerce-only conversion, self-checkout penetration, and operator replacement across museums, attractions, resorts, airports, and campuses would materially move o; lost-contract reason codes and procurement outcomes are especially valuable.
caveats: A vendor-reported renewal rate is not a direct measure of service quantity requiring an operator.
The cited operator is larger and more integrated than many LMM firms.
Demand quantity is handled in d5 and is not reduced again here.

narrative.executive_view: The coherent service business is outsourced experiential retail, not the typical standalone gift shop. A qualifying operator owns a venue partner's store, merchandise, people, systems, and fulfillment problem, producing recurring contracts and meaningful administrative and commercial workflows for AI; the universe is likely very small, and venue concentration, concession economics, and discretionary spending are central risks.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate assortment analysis, design ideation, product copy, localized marketing, purchasing support, forecasts, schedules, customer inquiries, e-commerce operations, and partner reporting. It does much less for physical store design, stocking, visual merchandising, packaging, guest interaction, construction, vendor execution, and accountable onsite leadership.
narrative.value_capture: Percentage-of-sales concession fees do not automatically share a labor saving, supporting initial retention. Minimum guarantees, public bids, contract renewals, venue demands, and consumer price competition eventually redistribute value, so durable benefit depends on contract tenure, renewal advantage, and differentiated operating quality.
narrative.firm_availability: The broad NAICS contains many standalone stores and microbusinesses that do not qualify. Turnkey external operators demonstrably exist, but no public source counts LMM firms with transferable venue contracts. General employer-owner surveys and broad retail transactions support only a proxy for five-year control transfer.
narrative.demand_durability: Real domestic leisure travel is forecast to grow and national-park visitation remains high, supporting destination retail. Museum recovery is uneven and gift purchases remain discretionary; lower visitor conversion, consumer trade-down, tariffs, weak inbound travel, or a shift from merchandise to experiences can offset attendance growth.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The principal uncertainties are classification leakage, a very low eligible share, absent firm-count data, reliance on one scaled operator's claims, and the lack of contract or operating benchmarks across venues. A transaction can be unattractive if one venue dominates revenue, change of control triggers rebidding, guarantees are aggressive, inventory ages quickly, or merchandise relevance depends on founder taste.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring venue partnerships with automatable merchandise, planning, e-commerce, and reporting work layered over durable physical operations.
narrative.principal_weakness: Only a small fraction of a transactional, discretionary retail NAICS appears to provide a qualifying outsourced service.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition: Gift, Novelty, and Souvenir Retailers
sources.supports: Defines 459420 as establishments primarily retailing new gifts, novelty merchandise, souvenirs, greeting cards, seasonal and holiday decorations, and curios; supports treating ordinary merchandise retail as distinct from outsourced operation.

sources.title: Data USA: Gift, novelty, and souvenir shops
sources.supports: Reports a 2022 workforce of 137,931 and identifies 40,718 retail salespersons, 30,939 first-line supervisors of retail sales workers, and 16,916 cashiers as the three largest occupations.

sources.title: O*NET Retail Salespersons job duties
sources.supports: Lists tasks including customer-needs discovery, recommendations, payments, product explanation, displays, inventory requisition, returns, security, special orders, records, contracts, packaging, and delivery arrangements.

sources.title: Event Network: What We Do
sources.supports: Documents a turnkey outsourced attraction-retail model with more than 100 partnerships and a vendor-reported 97% renewal rate, covering operations, staff, product development, purchasing, merchandising, design, e-commerce, fulfillment, technology, and partner collaboration.

sources.title: Event Network: Experiential retail solutions
sources.supports: Describes outsourced retail services for zoos, aquariums, art and science museums, natural-history and historic sites, hospitality gift shops, tourist destinations, and campuses, including bespoke brand-linked shopping experiences.

sources.title: U.S. Travel Forecast, Spring 2026
sources.supports: Projects inflation-adjusted travel spending growth of 1% in 2026 and 3% in 2027 and 2028; reports $909 billion of domestic leisure spending in 2026, an extended forecast through 2030, and elevated consumer and geopolitical risks.

sources.title: National Park Service: 2025 visitation statistics
sources.supports: Reports more than 323 million recreation visits and over 13 million overnight stays in 2025, with 26 parks setting visitation records; supports current attraction traffic, not gift-shop conversion.

sources.title: American Alliance of Museums 2024 Annual National Snapshot
sources.supports: Survey of more than 400 museum directors covering attendance and finances reports that recovery remains uneven and many museums have not returned to pre-pandemic attendance or financial health.

sources.title: Gallup: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports that 52.3% of US employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older and that 22% of employer-business owners in its fall-2024 survey planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years; plans are not completed qualifying transfers.

sources.title: BizBuySell retail business valuation benchmarks
sources.supports: Documents completed transactions for broad, mostly independent retail businesses from 2021-2025 and reports a five-year average earnings multiple of 2.62; establishes transfer-market activity but not an industry-specific probability.

sources.title: Griffith Observatory Bookstore and Gift Shop Concession Agreement
sources.supports: Public agreement sets tiered revenue-sharing fees at 25%, 30%, 35%, and 38% of gross receipts across annual-sales thresholds, illustrating direct venue participation in sales economics.

sources.title: Durango-La Plata County Airport Terminal Gift Shop Retail Concession Agreement, 2025
sources.supports: Specifies operator responsibility for costs and service standards, street-pricing constraints, and annual payment of the greater of a minimum guarantee or a percentage of gross gift-shop revenue.
